quiz.title: Algebra 2: Unit 14 Quiz 10
quiz.subtitle: 14.10: Matrix Operations in Real-World Contexts
quiz.subject: Mathematics
quiz.topic: Applying matrix math to inventory and economics

questions.question: A store sells 25 cookies, 15 brownies, and 30 muffins at Location 1; and 20 cookies, 25 brownies, and 10 muffins at Location 2. Write as a $2 \times 3$ matrix $S$. Then multiply by prices $P = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}$ to find revenue at each location.
January stock $J = \begin{bmatrix} 50 & 30 \\ 40 & 25 \end{bmatrix}$, February stock $F = \begin{bmatrix} 35 & 20 \\ 45 & 30 \end{bmatrix}$. Find the inventory change $F - J$.
A store has a $1 \times 3$ matrix of quantities sold and a $3 \times 1$ matrix of prices per item. What does their multiplication produce?
options: A $3 \times 3$ matrix representing price per item.
questions.explanation: $(1 \times 3) \times (3 \times 1) = 1 \times 1$. The dot product gives total revenue: sum of (quantity $\times$ price).

questions.question: A price matrix is $P = \begin{bmatrix} 20 & 35 & 15 \\ 40 & 25 & 30 \end{bmatrix}$. Apply a 15% discount to find the new prices.
questions.explanation: Multiply each element by $0.85$ (paying 85% of original price). $20 \times 0.85 = 17$, etc.

questions.question: Factory A uses materials $\begin{bmatrix} 100 & 200 \\ 150 & 50 \end{bmatrix}$ and labor $\begin{bmatrix} 40 & 60 \\ 35 & 25 \end{bmatrix}$. Find the total resource matrix.

Meal 1: 500 cal, 20g fat, 30g protein. Meal 2: 700 cal, 35g fat, 25g protein. You eat 3 of Meal 1 and 2 of Meal 2. Use $Q = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$, $N = \begin{bmatrix} 500 & 20 & 30 \\ 700 & 35 & 25 \end{bmatrix}$. Find total nutrition.
Matrix $N = \begin{bmatrix} 450 & 18 & 32 \\ 620 & 25 & 28 \end{bmatrix}$ shows (Calories, Fat, Protein) for 2 meals. What is $a_{23}$ and what does it represent?
A factory's production matrix is $M = \begin{bmatrix} 200 & 150 \\ 100 & 300 \end{bmatrix}$. Production must triple. Find $3M$.
questions.explanation: Scalar multiplication: multiply every element by $3$. $200 \times 3 = 600$, etc.

questions.question: An order: 4 chairs (15 lbs each), 2 tables (50 lbs each), 1 bookshelf (80 lbs). Use $Q = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $W = \begin{bmatrix} 15 \\ 50 \\ 80 \end{bmatrix}$ to find total weight.
A flight network matrix $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ (1 = direct flight). How many direct flights from City 2 to other cities?
questions.explanation: Row 2: $[1, 0, 0]$. Sum = 1. City 2 has a direct flight only to City 1.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You can apply matrix operations to complex real-world systems — revenue calculation, inventory analysis, discount pricing, resource management, nutrition tracking, production scaling, and network analysis.
scoreMessages.70: Good work. Key reminders: (1) Matrix multiplication (quantity × price) gives total revenue or weight, (2) Subtraction shows changes in inventory, (3) Scalar multiplication applies discounts or scaling, (4) Element $a_{ij}$ represents the value at row $i$, column $j$.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing. Focus on: (1) Setting up matrices with rows and columns representing the right categories, (2) Using the dot product for totals, (3) Matrix addition for combining resources, (4) Interpreting matrix entries in context.
scoreMessages.0: Review real-world matrix applications: (1) Organize data with rows and columns representing different categories, (2) Multiply a row vector (quantities) by a column vector (prices/weights) to get totals, (3) Add matrices to combine resources from different sources, (4) Scalar multiplication scales all entries — use for discounts or production changes.
